title: Module 1: Internet and Blockchain Foundations
description: Learn the evolution of the Web (1.0→2.0→3.0), blockchain basics, and consensus mechanisms (PoW/PoS).

lessons.title: The Evolution of the Web: 1.0 → 2.0 → 3.0
lessons.content:
## 강의 영상: 책에는 없는 Web3 1회 — AI 시대, 우리가 Web3에 주목해야 하는 이유

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bh6f13JJwe0

## Lecture Introduction

This lecture is based on a blockchain fundamentals course delivered by Minhyun Kim (founder of Common Computer, kimminhyun@comcom.ai). Kim has experience as a software engineer in search, distributed processing, machine learning, and user modeling, and has served as instructor and mentor at Open Source hackathons, Machine Learning Crash Course, Jeju ML Code Camp, and Google Impact Challenge.

References: https://a16z.com/crypto / https://a16z.com/crypto-startup-school/

---

## Web Basics

### What Is the Internet?

The internet is built on a **Server-Client architecture**. When you visit a website, your computer (Client) sends a request to a server (Server), which sends back a response. The browser makes this communication possible.

### What Is the Web?

An **HTTP-based information delivery system**. The first website was created at CERN in 1991: http://info.cern.ch/hypertext/WWW/TheProject.html

### What Is a Computer?

A computer is an **implementation of a Turing Machine** -- a machine that processes input and produces output according to given rules. This concept connects directly to blockchain's "State Machine."

---

## The Three Eras of the Web

### Web 1.0: Read-Only (1990s)

The first generation of the Web was essentially a digital library. Static HTML pages presented information, and users could only **read** content. Think of early Yahoo!, GeoCities, and personal homepages. There was no user interaction beyond clicking links.

### Web 2.0: Read + Write, but Stateless (2000s-present)

Web 2.0 brought interactivity. Users could now **create** content: blog posts, social media updates, YouTube videos. However, the underlying protocols (HTTP, IP) are **Stateless** -- servers do not inherently remember who you are between requests.

> "Examples of stateless protocols include the Internet Protocol (IP), which is the foundation for the Internet, and the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP), which is the foundation of data communication for the World Wide Web."

To solve this, platforms invented cookies, sessions, and databases to track users. But here is the critical insight: **this state data lives on platform servers**. Facebook knows your friends, Google knows your searches, Amazon knows your purchases. The platforms own the data, and this ownership became the source of their enormous power (GAFA: Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon).

### Web 3.0: Read + Write + Own, Stateful (emerging)

Web 3.0 introduces a paradigm shift: the internet itself becomes **Stateful** through blockchain. Key characteristics:

- **Stateful Internet**: Blockchain permanently records and maintains state
- **Programmable Internet**: Smart contracts make the internet programmable
- **Internet Computer / Blockchain Computer**: A computer shared by the entire world

Without central servers, the whole world can reach consensus on "how much is in my account."

## Open Source to Open Resource

The transition from Open Source to **Open Resource** represents a fundamentally new economic model:

| | Open Source | Open Resource |
|---|---|---|
| What is shared | Source code | Code + deployed service |
| Revenue | Indirect (support, SaaS) | Direct (automatic revenue sharing) |
| Deployment | User responsibility | Immediately available |
| Example | Linux kernel | Blockchain protocol |

In the Web 2.0 era, open source code had to be packaged and hosted by companies to become products. In Web 3.0, **code IS the product** -- deploying a smart contract creates a service anyone can use, and the protocol automatically distributes revenue to contributors.

## Then & Now: Great Vessels Are Completed Slowly

The early internet (1990s) was dismissed as a toy -- slow modems, ugly pages, limited use cases. Yet within 15 years it transformed every industry. Blockchain faces similar skepticism today. The pattern of **"great vessels are completed slowly"** repeats: technologies that seem underwhelming at launch can achieve extraordinary outcomes through persistent compound growth.
key_ideas: Web 1.0 (read-only) → Web 2.0 (read+write, Stateless) → Web 3.0 (read+write+own, Stateful)
Core feature of Web 2.0: HTTP, IP are Stateless Protocols — servers don't remember state
Core feature of Web 3.0: Stateful Internet — blockchain permanently maintains and updates state
Open Source → Open Resource transition: code sharing enables immediate service deployment with usage-based revenue sharing
Ownership revolution: In Web 2.0 platforms own data, in Web 3.0 users own their data directly
lessons.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 01
lessons.exercise:
What is the most fundamental technical difference between Web 2.0 and Web 3.0?
1) Transition from centralized servers to distributed cloud infrastructure
2) Transition from Stateless to Stateful
3) Transition from read-write to read-write-execute via smart APIs
4) Transition from HTTP protocol to a new blockchain-native protocol
lessons.explanation: Web 2.0's HTTP and IP protocols are Stateless (don't store state). Servers don't remember users, so we had to artificially manage state with cookies and sessions. Platforms owning this data became their source of power. Web 3.0's blockchain is Stateful (stores state) -- the blockchain itself permanently records all transactions and states. Ownership, decentralization, DeFi -- all Web 3.0 innovations are results made possible by this Stateful characteristic.

lessons.title: Blockchain Fundamentals
lessons.content:
## What is a Blockchain?

At its core, a blockchain is a **tamper-proof ledger shared globally** -- a State Machine that records every transaction and maintains a consistent view of the world across thousands of computers.

## Two Core Technologies of Blockchain

### Asymmetric Cryptography: The Key Pair

Blockchain security rests on **asymmetric encryption**. Every user has two mathematically linked keys:

- **Private key**: A secret key only you know. Used to **sign** transactions, proving you authorized them.
- **Public key**: Derived from the private key but shareable with anyone. Used to **verify** that a signature is genuine.

**Analogy**: Your private key is like a wax seal stamp that only you possess. Your public key is like the pattern the seal leaves -- anyone can verify the pattern matches your stamp, but no one can forge the stamp itself.

### Hash Functions: Digital Fingerprints

A hash function takes any input and produces a fixed-length "fingerprint." Even changing a single bit completely changes the output. Key properties:

- **Deterministic**: Same input always produces the same output
- **One-way**: Cannot reverse-engineer the input from the hash
- **Collision-resistant**: Practically impossible to find two inputs with the same hash

## How the Chain Forms

In blockchain, each block contains the **hash of the previous block**, forming a chain:

```
Block #99 → hash(Block #99) stored in Block #100 → hash(Block #100) stored in Block #101...
```

If you tamper with Block #99, its hash changes, which no longer matches what Block #100 recorded. This inconsistency cascades through every subsequent block. To successfully forge history, you would need to recompute **all subsequent blocks** faster than the entire network adds new ones -- practically impossible.

## Blockchain as a State Machine

Blockchain operates as a global State Machine:

- **State S**: Current snapshot (Alice has 100 tokens, Bob has 50)
- **Transaction TX**: Input that changes state (Alice sends 30 to Bob)
- **New State S'**: Result after applying input (Alice has 70, Bob has 80)

Ethereum formalizes this: `APPLY(S, TX) = S'`

This is conceptually identical to a Turing Machine: given a current state and an input, produce a new state. The difference is that this state machine runs on thousands of computers simultaneously, and they all agree on the same result.

## EOA (Externally Owned Account)

Transactions on blockchain require signing with a **private key**. This is the principle behind EOA (Externally Owned Account): only the person holding the private key outside the blockchain can control the account.

Key properties of EOA:
- Controlled by a private key, not by code
- Can initiate transactions
- No bank, no government, no platform can move your assets without your private key
- "Not your keys, not your coins" -- if you lose your private key, you lose access permanently
key_ideas: Blockchain = A tamper-proof ledger shared globally (State Machine)
Asymmetric encryption: Private key (signing) + Public key (verification) for transaction authentication
Hash function: Including previous block's fingerprint in next block → chain formation, instant tampering detection
EOA (Externally Owned Accounts): Transactions only possible with private key signature
Core blockchain property: Data manipulation is extremely difficult (immutability)
lessons.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 01
lessons.exercise:
How is the 'chain' formed in blockchain?
1) Each block contains the hash value of the previous block
2) Each block references the next block's public key for sequential verification
3) Blocks are linked by shared nonce values from the consensus process
4) Blocks are ordered by their Merkle root signatures in a global ledger
lessons.answer: Each block contains the hash value of the previous block
lessons.explanation: A hash function transforms any data into a fixed-length fingerprint. Even changing 1 bit completely changes the hash value. Each block contains the hash value of the previous block, forming the chain. If you manipulate a past block, that block's hash changes, causing a mismatch with the next block's stored hash. This cascades through every subsequent block, making tampering practically detectable and the chain immutable.

lessons.title: Consensus Mechanisms: PoW vs PoS
lessons.content:
## The Consensus Problem

In a decentralized network with no central authority, how do thousands of computers agree on who has the right to write the next block? This is the **consensus problem**, and two major solutions have emerged.

## Proof of Work (PoW): Bitcoin's Method

PoW works like a math competition:

### How It Works
1. Thousands of miners race to solve a cryptographic puzzle (finding a hash with a specific number of leading zeros)
2. The **first to solve it** earns the right to create the next block
3. The winner receives a block reward (newly minted coins)
4. Other nodes verify the solution (easy to verify, hard to find) and accept the block

### Why It's Secure
The "work" is the massive computational effort required. This cost makes attacking the network economically irrational -- honest mining is more profitable than cheating.

**Analogy**: Imagine a lottery where tickets cost real electricity. The winner gets to write the next page of the global ledger. Cheating would mean wasting all that electricity for nothing.

## Proof of Stake (PoS): Ethereum's Method since 2022

PoS replaces computation with economic collateral:

### How It Works
1. Validators **stake** coins as a security deposit (32 ETH minimum for Ethereum)
2. The protocol randomly selects a validator to propose the next block
3. Other validators verify and attest to the block
4. Honest validators earn staking rewards; dishonest ones get **slashed** (their stake is confiscated)

### Why It's More Efficient
No massive computational waste. Security comes from the economic cost of losing staked assets, not from burning electricity.

## PoW vs PoS Comparison

| Aspect | PoW | PoS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Security source | Computational work | Economic collateral |
| Energy usage | Very high | Very low (~99.95% less) |
| Hardware | Specialized ASICs | Standard servers |
| Attack cost | Buy mining hardware | Buy and stake coins |
| Example | Bitcoin | Ethereum (post-Merge) |

## Nothing at Stake Problem and Slashing

In PoW, mining on two chains means splitting your hashpower (costly). In PoS, voting on two chains costs nothing -- just clicking twice. This **Nothing at Stake** problem is solved by **Slashing**: if caught double-voting, your staked coins are confiscated. This transforms "nothing at stake" into "everything at stake."

### How Slashing Works
- Validators must sign attestations with their private key
- Double-signing is cryptographically detectable
- Penalty: partial or total confiscation of staked ETH
- This creates a strong economic disincentive against cheating

## Then & Now: The Merge (September 2022)

Ethereum's transition from PoW to PoS was historic -- the largest blockchain network upgrade ever, executed live without stopping the network.

### Key Facts
- Energy consumption reduced by **99.95%**
- Eliminated the environmental criticism that plagued proof-of-work chains
- Over $40 billion in staked ETH secured the network post-Merge
- Validators replaced miners as the network's security providers

### What It Proved
The Merge demonstrated that a major decentralized network could fundamentally change its consensus mechanism while running -- something many believed impossible. It set a precedent for future blockchain upgrades.
key_ideas: Consensus mechanism: Rules to decide 'who has the right to write the next block' without a central authority
PoW (Proof of Work): First to solve a math puzzle creates the block — Bitcoin's method
PoS (Proof of Stake): Random selection from those who stake coins — Ethereum's method (since 2022)
The Merge (2022): Ethereum's PoW→PoS transition, 99.95% energy reduction
Nothing at Stake problem and Slashing: PoS weakness and its solution
lessons.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 01
lessons.exercise:
What is the 'work' in PoW (Proof of Work)?
1) Computer calculations solving math problems
2) Staking cryptocurrency tokens as collateral for block validation
3) Verifying digital signatures of all transactions in the mempool
4) Broadcasting transaction data across the peer-to-peer network
lessons.explanation: PoW requires miners to solve a cryptographic puzzle: finding a nonce that, when combined with block data and hashed, produces a result starting with a certain number of zeros. This is pure trial-and-error computation that consumes massive electricity. The first to find a valid solution wins the right to create the block and receive the reward. This cost is what makes attacking Bitcoin more expensive than cooperating.

lessons.title: The Double Spending Problem and Necessity of Consensus
lessons.content:
## The Double Spending Problem

Digital data is trivially copyable. If you send a music file to a friend, you still have a copy. What if you could do the same with money? This is the **double spending problem**: attempting to spend the same digital money twice.

## The Centralized Solution: Banks

Banks solve this easily: a central server tracks balances. When you send $100 to Alice, the bank deducts from your account first. If you try to send the same $100 to Bob, the bank rejects it -- insufficient funds.

### The Trade-off
This works, but requires **trusting** the bank, which can:
- Freeze accounts arbitrarily
- Impose fees
- Restrict access
- Be subject to government pressure

## The Decentralization Challenge

Without a central authority, who decides which transaction is valid? You might think: "Just check timestamps to see which transaction came first!" This fails for three reasons:

### Why Time-Based Ordering Fails
1. **No central clock**: Thousands of computers worldwide have slightly different clocks, and clocks can be manipulated
2. **Network delays**: A transaction from Korea to the US takes ~0.1 seconds; during that time, conflicting transactions can occur
3. **Simultaneous attack**: An attacker can deliberately broadcast two conflicting transactions at the exact same moment

**Analogy**: Two people in different countries both claim they called you first. Without a central phone log, whose local time do you trust?

## The PoW/PoS Solution: Qualification, Not Time

Satoshi Nakamoto's insight was revolutionary: **don't ask "which came first?" -- ask "who gets to write?"**

PoW/PoS select ONE authority per block through competition (PoW) or lottery (PoS). That authority's ordering becomes the truth:

1. One person earns the right to write a block
2. They record "Alice gets the $100" in the block
3. All nodes worldwide copy this record
4. Your balance is now $0 globally
5. The transaction to Bob? Rejected -- insufficient funds

## Economic Security: Why Attacking Is Hard

Attacking the network requires controlling 51%+ of mining power (PoW) or staked coins (PoS) -- costing billions of dollars.

### The Attack Paradox
Even if successful, the attack would crash the coin's value, destroying the attacker's own investment. **Attack cost > attack benefit**, so rational actors cooperate.

### The Numbers
- Bitcoin 51% attack cost: Estimated $10B+ in mining hardware
- Ethereum 51% attack cost: Requires 51% of ~30M staked ETH (~$50B+)
- Both figures make attacks economically irrational

## Trustless Trust

You don't trust people. You trust economic incentive structures. This is **Trustless Trust** -- the system is designed so that cheating is more expensive than cooperating, making trust in individuals unnecessary.

This is blockchain's fundamental breakthrough: replacing social trust with mathematical and economic guarantees.
key_ideas: Double spending: Attempting to pay twice with the same money
Why time order doesn't work: No central clock in distributed systems
The real question: Without a central authority, how do we decide which transaction came first?
PoW/PoS solution: Don't determine by time, determine by qualification — only one person records
lessons.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture
lessons.exercise:
What is the 'double spending' problem?
1) Attempting to pay twice with the same money
2) Miners charging duplicate fees for processing the same transaction
3) Transactions being confirmed on two different forks simultaneously
4) Users accidentally sending funds to the wrong address twice
lessons.answer: Attempting to pay twice with the same money
lessons.explanation: Double spending means trying to spend the same digital money twice -- for example, sending $100 to Alice and simultaneously sending the same $100 to Bob. In the physical world, this is impossible (you hand cash to Alice, you no longer have it). In the digital world, data is trivially copyable. Blockchain solves this through consensus mechanisms: PoW/PoS select one authority per block who determines the canonical transaction ordering, making double spending impossible.

title: Module 2: Decentralization and Platform Economy
description: Learn Chris Dixon's platform lifecycle theory, the bait-and-switch problem, Voice and Exit solutions, and the Wikipedia vs Encarta case study.

lessons.title: The Lifecycle of Centralized Platforms
lessons.content:
## Source: Why Decentralization Matters

In his 2018 article "Why Decentralization Matters," **Chris Dixon** (partner at a16z crypto) identified a predictable lifecycle that centralized platforms follow. This framework explains why even well-intentioned platforms eventually exploit their users.

Reference: https://onezero.medium.com/why-decentralization-matters-5e3f79f7638e

## The Two Eras of the Internet

The internet can be divided into two distinct eras:

### Era 1 (1980s-2000s): Open Protocols
- Governed by the community: HTTP, SMTP, DNS
- Decentralized and open
- Anyone could build on them
- Value accrued to the edges (users and builders)

### Era 2 (2000s-present): Centralized Platforms
- GAFA (Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon) built on top of open protocols
- Captured users with superior products
- Gradually extracted value from the ecosystem
- Shifted power from users to shareholders

The transition happened because centralized platforms could iterate faster and provide better user experiences than open protocols. But this speed came at the cost of user sovereignty.

## The Predictable 3-Stage Lifecycle of Centralized Platforms

### Stage 1: Attract
"Come build on our platform! It's free!"

The platform offers tremendous value to users, developers, and businesses. APIs are free, fees are zero, and the platform actively nurtures its ecosystem. This is a **positive-sum** relationship where both the platform and participants benefit.

### Stage 2: Grow
Network effects kick in. Users attract more users. Switching costs rise as people invest time, content, and relationships into the platform. The platform becomes essential infrastructure -- everyone uses Google because everyone uses Google. Platform power over users and third parties steadily increases.

### Stage 3: Extract
"Now you can't live without us."

The relationship shifts from **positive-sum to zero-sum**. The platform raises fees, changes rules, competes with its own developers, and extracts maximum value. Shareholders demand quarterly growth, and when new user acquisition slows, the only option is extracting more from existing users.

## Google Case Study: Don't Be Evil to Antitrust Ruling

### The Timeline
- **2004**: IPO letter declares "Don't be evil" and promises to prioritize long-term user value
- **2010s**: Gradually prioritizes ads over organic search results
- **2018**: "Don't be evil" quietly removed from Code of Conduct
- **2024**: US federal court rules Google **illegally monopolized** search, citing billions in payments to be the default search engine

### The Lesson
Google's trajectory perfectly illustrates the 3-stage lifecycle. The founders genuinely meant "Don't be evil." But structural incentives -- shareholder pressure, advertising revenue model, monopoly power -- made extraction inevitable.

**Analogy**: Think of it like a landlord who offers cheap rent to fill an empty building, then raises rent once everyone's moved in and has nowhere else to go. The incentive structure guarantees exploitation once lock-in is achieved.

## Then & Now

Chris Dixon wrote this analysis in 2018. Since then:
- **Meta (2021)**: Rebranded, pivoted to metaverse, increased ad load across platforms
- **Twitter/X (2022-23)**: New ownership dramatically changed API pricing and platform rules
- **Google (2024)**: Lost landmark antitrust case
- **Apple (2024)**: Forced by EU to allow alternative app stores (DMA regulation)

The pattern keeps repeating, validating Dixon's framework. The question is: can Web3 break the cycle?
key_ideas: Chris Dixon's 3-stage platform lifecycle: Attract → Grow → Extract
Attract: Offer value to users and developers to build the platform
Grow: Platform becomes indispensable, network effects lock users in
Extract: Change rules, raise fees, exploit users — Positive-sum → Zero-sum
Google example: 'Don't be evil' → Antitrust ruling
lessons.exercise:
What are the three stages in Chris Dixon's centralized platform lifecycle?
1) Attract → Grow → Extract
2) Onboard → Monetize → Regulate
3) Subsidize → Scale → Monopolize
4) Innovate → Dominate → Decentralize
lessons.explanation: Chris Dixon identified a predictable 3-stage lifecycle: Attract (offer value, positive-sum), Grow (network effects, lock-in), and Extract (change rules, zero-sum). This is driven by structural incentives -- shareholder pressure demands growth, and once user acquisition slows, extracting from existing users becomes the rational strategy. Google's journey from 'Don't be evil' to antitrust ruling illustrates this trajectory.

lessons.title: Bait-and-Switch: Platform Betrayal
lessons.content:
## What is Bait-and-Switch?

Bait-and-switch is the pattern where platforms **attract** users and developers with favorable terms, then **change the rules** once lock-in is complete. It is the Extract stage in action.

This is not limited to tech -- it's a fundamental pattern of centralized power. But in the digital world, network effects make it particularly devastating.

## Historical Bait-and-Switch Cases

Chris Dixon documented several cases in "Why Decentralization Matters":

### Microsoft vs Netscape (1990s)
Microsoft distributed Internet Explorer for free and bundled it with Windows, destroying Netscape's 90% browser market share. Netscape was eventually acquired by AOL and development ceased in 2008.

### Twitter vs Third-Party Clients
Twitter opened its API ("Build great Twitter apps!"), fostering a thriving ecosystem including Tweetbot and Twitterrific. Then it gradually restricted API access, limited third-party features, and in 2023 priced the API at $42,000/month -- killing most independent apps.

### App Store "Apple Tax"
Apple and Google invited developers to build apps for billions of users, then imposed a **30% commission** on all revenue, arbitrarily rejected apps, and absorbed popular third-party features into the OS (flashlight apps became a built-in feature).

### Google Maps API Pricing
Google Maps started free for developers, becoming the default mapping solution. In 2018, Google imposed aggressive pricing -- some developers saw bills increase by **1,400%** overnight.

## Lock-in Effect

By the time the "switch" happens, developers have:
- Years of code built on platform-specific APIs
- Customers who only use that platform
- Reputations and followers that can't be migrated
- Too much investment to start over elsewhere

### Why Lock-in Is So Effective
Switching costs are not just technical but also social and economic. A YouTube creator with 1 million subscribers cannot simply move to another platform -- the audience doesn't follow automatically.

This creates an **asymmetric power relationship**: the platform can unilaterally change terms, and users have no viable alternative.

## The Result: Innovation Stagnation

The long-term consequence is devastating: the best entrepreneurs, developers, and investors learn **not to build on centralized platforms**. Innovation stagnates because talented people choose safer paths rather than building on quicksand.

**Analogy**: Imagine a farmer who plants crops on rented land, only to have the landlord change the lease terms after harvest season. Next time, the farmer will think twice about planting anything at all.

### The Chilling Effect
This creates a vicious cycle:
1. Platform extracts value from developers
2. New developers avoid the platform
3. Platform has less innovation to offer users
4. Platform extracts even more from remaining developers

## YouTube Creators: A Living Example

YouTube creators illustrate bait-and-switch in real time:

### The Bait
"Broadcast yourself!" -- YouTube offered free hosting, global reach, and ad revenue sharing. Millions of creators invested years building audiences.

### The Switch
- **Algorithm changes**: Views can drop 80% overnight due to recommendation algorithm changes
- **Demonetization**: Entire categories of content lose revenue without clear explanation
- **Revenue split**: YouTube takes 45% of ad revenue, with opaque calculations
- **Content ID abuse**: False copyright claims can freeze a creator's income

### Why They Can't Leave
A creator with millions of subscribers, years of content, and an audience trained to use YouTube has no realistic exit option. Moving to another platform means starting from zero.
key_ideas: Bait: Attract with free access, open APIs, favorable terms
Switch: Once locked in, change rules, raise fees, restrict access
Result: Developers learn not to build on centralized platforms
Historical examples: Twitter API, Facebook platform, Google search
lessons.exercise:
What is 'bait-and-switch' in the context of platforms?
1) Attracting users with good terms then changing rules once they're locked in
2) Offering open APIs initially then charging high licensing fees for continued access
3) Acquiring competing startups and merging their user bases into one platform
4) Launching a decentralized version of the platform to avoid regulatory scrutiny
lessons.answer: Attracting users with good terms then changing rules once they're locked in
lessons.explanation: Bait-and-switch is the pattern where platforms first attract developers and users with favorable terms (free APIs, low fees, open ecosystems), then change the rules once users are locked in and switching costs are too high to leave. Twitter raising API prices to $42,000/month and Apple imposing 30% commissions are classic examples. This is why decentralization matters -- smart contracts and open source code prevent unilateral rule changes.

lessons.title: Voice and Exit: Solutions Through Decentralization
lessons.content:
## Exit, Voice, and Loyalty Theory

Economist **Albert O. Hirschman** proposed in his 1970 book "Exit, Voice, and Loyalty" that when people are dissatisfied with an organization, they have two options:

- **Voice**: Express dissatisfaction and try to change the system from within
- **Exit**: Leave the system entirely
- **Loyalty**: The factor that determines whether people choose voice or exit

Chris Dixon applied this framework to explain why decentralized networks solve the platform problem.

## Centralized Platforms: Neither Voice Nor Exit

### No Effective Voice
- If you dislike Twitter's policies, you cannot vote to change them
- Customer support ignores complaints
- Individual shareholders have negligible influence
- Platform executives make unilateral decisions
- Even large creators have limited bargaining power

### Costly Exit
- Leaving Instagram means losing years of followers, photos, and social connections
- Switching costs are not just technical but social
- The switching cost is so high that users tolerate worsening conditions
- Data portability is limited or non-existent

## Cryptonetworks: Both Voice AND Exit

### Voice through Governance
Token holders vote on protocol changes. Real examples:
- **Uniswap**: UNI holders control a $3B+ treasury
- **MakerDAO**: Community voted to rebrand to Sky in 2024
- **Compound**: COMP holders set interest rate models
- **Aave**: Governance proposals control billions in protocol parameters

You are a **citizen**, not just a customer.

### Exit through Portability
- Your assets live in your wallet, not on a platform server
- You can take your tokens and NFTs to any compatible service
- On-chain data is publicly accessible, enabling true data portability
- No platform can "lock" your assets (self-custody)

## Fork: The Ultimate Exit

Blockchain code is **open source**. If you disagree with the rules, you can copy the entire system and start your own version:

### Historical Forks
- **Ethereum Classic (2016)**: Community split over DAO hack response -- one chain rolled back, the other preserved immutability
- **Bitcoin Cash (2017)**: Disagreement over block size -- larger blocks vs. original design
- **SushiSwap (2020)**: "Vampire attack" -- forked Uniswap's code and used token incentives to attract liquidity

### The Power of the Fork Threat
The **threat of fork** keeps network operators honest. If they exploit users, users fork and leave -- taking the code with them. This is a power dynamic that simply doesn't exist in centralized platforms.

## Four Mechanisms That Prevent Bait-and-Switch

1. **Code is law**: Rules are in smart contracts, not policy documents -- they can't be changed unilaterally
2. **Open source**: Anyone can verify, audit, and copy the code
3. **Fork threat**: Exploitation triggers user exodus with a clone
4. **Token alignment**: Network value = token value, so operators who harm users harm themselves

## Aligned Incentives

The deepest insight is about **incentive alignment**. In centralized platforms, shareholder interests diverge from user interests over time. In cryptonetworks:

- Protocol token holders ARE the users
- Improving the network increases token value
- Harming users decreases token value
- **Self-interest and collective interest are aligned**

**Analogy**: Imagine if unhappy Instagram users could copy ALL of Instagram's code, ALL user data, and run their own version. Centralized platforms would never exploit users if this were possible. In Web3, it IS possible.
key_ideas: Voice: Governance voting allows rule changes through democratic process
Exit: Can leave at no cost, fork is possible
Why decentralization prevents bait-and-switch: Code is law, open source, fork threat, token incentives
Fork: If unhappy with rules, copy the entire system and run your own version
lessons.exercise:
What is 'Voice' in the context of decentralized platforms?
1) Ability to vote on governance and change rules
2) Ability to fork the codebase and create a competing network
3) Ability to transfer assets and data to another platform freely
4) Ability to submit bug reports and feature requests to core developers
lessons.answer: Ability to vote on governance and change rules
lessons.explanation: Voice in decentralized platforms means token holders can vote on governance proposals to change protocol rules. Unlike centralized platforms where executives make unilateral decisions, decentralized protocols give users democratic power -- Uniswap's UNI holders control a multi-billion dollar treasury, and MakerDAO's community voted to rebrand entirely. Combined with Exit (portability and fork capability), this structurally prevents the bait-and-switch pattern.

lessons.title: Wikipedia vs Encarta: Triumph of Decentralization
lessons.content:
## Why This Case Matters

The Wikipedia vs Encarta story is one of the most powerful demonstrations of decentralization's long-term advantage over centralization. It shows why **judging systems by their initial state is misleading** -- what matters is the growth trajectory.

This case study directly parallels the debate between centralized platforms and decentralized networks.

## Microsoft Encarta: The Pinnacle of Centralization

Microsoft Encarta launched in 1993 with massive corporate backing:

### Strengths
- Professional editors and fact-checkers
- Beautiful multimedia presentation
- Polished, comprehensive, and reliable from day one
- Annual DVD releases with curated updates

### The Outcome
- **2009**: 62,000 articles. Service discontinued.
- Could not compete with a volunteer-driven competitor
- Microsoft's centralized editorial model could not scale

## Wikipedia: The Triumph of Decentralization

Wikipedia launched in 2001 as a volunteer-driven experiment:

### Early Weaknesses
- Anyone could edit, no credentials required
- Initially rough, incomplete, and error-prone
- Critics scoffed: "How can amateurs compete with professionals?"

### The Results
- **2009**: 3,000,000+ articles. Growing exponentially.
- **2024**: 60+ million articles across 300+ languages
- Essential internet infrastructure used by billions

## Key Lesson: Static Products vs Dynamic Processes

The key insight is that **static snapshots are misleading**. In 2002, Encarta was objectively better than Wikipedia. But comparing products at a single point in time misses the dynamic: you should compare **processes**, not snapshots.

### What Changes When You Compare Processes
- A centralized product improves at the speed of its editorial team (linear)
- A decentralized process improves at the speed of its entire community (exponential)
- At some crossover point, exponential overtakes linear, and the gap only widens

## The Rate of Improvement

### Encarta's Growth Model
- Limited by Microsoft's budget for editors
- Content reviewed and approved through corporate hierarchy
- **Linear growth**: N editors = N articles per year

### Wikipedia's Growth Model
- Unlimited potential contributors
- Self-correcting through community review
- **Exponential growth**: Each contributor attracts more contributors
- Network effects in knowledge creation

At some crossover point, the exponential curve overtook the linear one, and the gap has only widened since.

## Use vs Contribution

A key distinction between centralized and decentralized systems:

| Aspect | Encarta (Centralized) | Wikipedia (Decentralized) |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Users | Consumers only | Consumers AND contributors |
| Motivation | Salary (employees) | Passion (volunteers) |
| Quality control | Top-down editorial | Bottom-up community review |
| Scalability | Limited by budget | Limited only by interest |

In decentralized systems, **every user is a potential contributor**. This collapses the boundary between producer and consumer.

## Great Vessels Are Completed Slowly (Da Qi Wan Cheng)

The Chinese concept **"da qi wan cheng" (great vessels are completed slowly)** from the Dao De Jing (Chapter 41) captures this principle perfectly:

> "The great square has no corners; the great vessel is completed slowly; the great note has a faint sound; the great form has no shape."

### Applied to Technology
Great things appear incomplete or underwhelming at first, but achieve extraordinary outcomes through persistent compound growth:

- **Bitcoin (2009)**: "Internet play money" -> $1T+ asset class
- **Linux (1991)**: "Hobby OS" -> Runs 90%+ of cloud servers
- **Ethereum (2015)**: "Useless world computer" -> $100B+ DeFi ecosystem
- **Open source** generally: "Free = low quality" -> Powers most internet infrastructure

### The Broader Pattern
This pattern repeats across technology: Linux vs proprietary Unix, Bitcoin vs traditional finance, open source vs closed software. The decentralized version starts weaker but grows faster.

## Cryptonetworks' Compound Feedback Loop

Decentralized networks combine multiple exponential growth mechanisms:

1. **Developer flywheel**: More developers -> better tools -> more developers
2. **User flywheel**: More users -> more liquidity -> better prices -> more users
3. **Token flywheel**: More usage -> higher token value -> more incentive to contribute
4. **Knowledge flywheel**: More documentation -> lower learning curve -> more contributors

These loops compound on each other, creating growth that no centralized organization can match over the long term. This is why Chris Dixon argues: **"The next big thing will start out looking like a toy."**
key_ideas: Centralized (Encarta): High initial quality → improves at employee speed only → died
Decentralized (Wikipedia): Initially incomplete → explosive community growth → dominates
Dynamic process comparison: Centralized starts better but decentralized wins long-term
大器晚成 (Late blooming): Great vessels are completed slowly
lessons.paper_ref: Chris Dixon, 'Why Decentralization Matters' (2018) / 道德經 Chapter 41
lessons.exercise:
What happened to Microsoft Encarta vs Wikipedia?
1) Encarta maintained professional quality but couldn't scale, while Wikipedia matched it through paid editors
2) Wikipedia started poorly but grew to dominate, Encarta died
3) Both coexisted for decades until streaming media made encyclopedias obsolete
4) Encarta pivoted to a wiki model but failed to attract enough volunteer contributors
lessons.answer: Wikipedia started poorly but grew to dominate, Encarta died
lessons.explanation: Encarta started with professional quality but improved only at the speed of Microsoft's editorial team (linear). Wikipedia started rough but improved at the speed of millions of volunteers (exponential). By 2009, Wikipedia had 3M+ articles while Encarta had 62K and was discontinued. This demonstrates that decentralized systems with aligned incentives eventually outperform centralized ones through compound community growth -- the principle of 'great vessels are completed slowly.'

title: Module 3: Scalability and the Blockchain Ecosystem
description: Learn about the blockchain trilemma, Layer 2 scalability solutions, Ethereum and EVM, and the DeFi/NFT ecosystem.

lessons.title: Ethereum and Smart Contracts
lessons.content:
## Ethereum's Birth

In 2014, **Vitalik Buterin** published the Ethereum whitepaper with a radical question: "What if we could run ANY program on the blockchain?"

Bitcoin could only do simple transactions (send value from A to B). Buterin, then just 19 years old, proposed a blockchain that could execute arbitrary programs -- a **world computer**.

**Analogy**: Bitcoin is a calculator (addition and subtraction only). Ethereum is a full computer (any program you can imagine).

## Ethereum's Innovation: Turing-Complete Smart Contracts

### What Turing-Complete Means
A Turing-complete system can theoretically perform any computation given enough time and resources. This means Ethereum can run:
- **DeFi protocols**: Lending, trading, derivatives
- **NFT contracts**: Digital ownership and royalties
- **DAOs**: Decentralized organizations with code-based governance
- **Games, social networks**, and anything else programmable

### Why This Matters
Before Ethereum, each blockchain application needed its own blockchain. Ethereum provided a **shared platform** where anyone could deploy programs, just as the App Store let anyone deploy apps on iPhone.

This is why Ethereum is called the **"World Computer."**

## EVM: The Ethereum Virtual Machine

The EVM is a standardized execution environment that runs identically on every Ethereum node worldwide.

### Key Properties
- **Deterministic**: Given the same input, every node produces the **guaranteed same output** regardless of underlying hardware or operating system
- **Sandboxed**: Smart contracts cannot access the filesystem or network -- they can only interact with blockchain state
- **Stack-based**: Uses a stack-based architecture similar to JVM

### Why Determinism Matters
This deterministic execution is what enables **global consensus on computation**, not just data. When a smart contract executes, thousands of nodes worldwide run the same code and agree on the result. Without determinism, nodes would produce different outputs, and consensus would be impossible.

## Ethereum's Formula

The entire Ethereum state transition can be expressed as:

`APPLY(S, TX) = S'`

Where S is the current state, TX is a transaction, and S' is the new state. Simple but powerful.

## Smart Contracts: Code is Law

A smart contract is code deployed on blockchain that **executes automatically** when conditions are met. Unlike traditional contracts enforced by courts, smart contracts are enforced by code:

### How They Work
```
if (buyer sends 100 ETH) {
    transfer NFT ownership to buyer;
}
```

### Key Properties
- **Immutable**: Once deployed, the code cannot be changed
- **Deterministic**: Same input always produces same output
- **Trustless**: No need to trust a counterparty
- **Composable**: Contracts can call other contracts, like building blocks

### The "Code is Law" Principle
Traditional law: write a contract -> dispute arises -> go to court -> judge interprets
Smart contract: write code -> conditions met -> execution is automatic -> no interpretation needed

This eliminates the ambiguity and enforcement costs of traditional contracts.

## Gas: Computational Fuel

Turing-completeness introduces a danger: **infinite loops**. If someone deploys `while(true) {}`, it could freeze the entire network. Gas solves this:

### How Gas Works
- Every operation costs a measured amount of gas
- Users set a gas limit per transaction
- If gas runs out, the transaction reverts automatically
- Infinite loops simply consume gas until depleted, then stop

### Gas Economics
- Total cost = Gas Used x Gas Price (in gwei)
- A simple ETH transfer costs ~21,000 gas
- A complex DeFi swap might cost ~200,000 gas
- Gas prices fluctuate based on network demand (higher demand = higher prices)

## DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization)

### What is a DAO?
A DAO is an **organization governed by smart contracts** instead of human executives. Rules are written in code, and decisions are made through token-holder voting.

### How DAOs Work
1. Rules and treasury are managed by smart contracts
2. Token holders propose and vote on decisions
3. Approved proposals are executed automatically by code
4. No CEO, no board of directors -- code is the governance layer

### The DAO Hack (2016)
The first major DAO ("The DAO") raised $150M in ETH but was hacked due to a smart contract vulnerability. The community faced a dilemma:
- **Roll back**: Reverse the hack, violating immutability
- **Preserve**: Accept the hack, preserving "code is law"

The community chose to roll back, creating **Ethereum** (rolled back) and **Ethereum Classic** (preserved). This event sparked the fundamental debate about whether "code is law" should be absolute or whether human governance should override code in extreme cases.

### Modern DAOs
Today, DAOs manage billions of dollars: MakerDAO, Uniswap, Aave, and hundreds of others. They represent a new form of human organization -- transparent, programmable, and globally accessible.
key_ideas: Ethereum innovation: Turing-complete smart contracts — programmable blockchain
EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine): A virtual machine that runs identically across the entire world
Gas: Computational cost, defense against malicious code (infinite loops)
Smart contracts: Code that executes automatically when conditions are met
lessons.exercise:
What is Ethereum's main innovation compared to Bitcoin?
1) A more efficient UTXO model that reduces transaction confirmation times
2) Turing-complete smart contracts (programmable blockchain)
3) A delegated proof-of-stake system that lowers energy consumption
4) Native support for token standards that enable cross-chain interoperability
lessons.explanation: Bitcoin can only perform simple scripted transactions (send value A to B). Ethereum introduced Turing-complete smart contracts, meaning any computation can be programmed and executed on the blockchain. This enabled DeFi, NFTs, DAOs, games, and any conceivable application. Vitalik Buterin's insight was to evolve blockchain from 'decentralized money' (Bitcoin) to 'decentralized computation' (Ethereum).

lessons.title: Layer 2: Solving Scalability
lessons.content:
## Chris Dixon's Prediction (2018)

In "Why Decentralization Matters," Chris Dixon wrote in 2018:

> "It's early enough that there's still time to develop new platforms that take a different approach. We'll see if we can construct a better internet."

His thesis: the next few years would focus on building the **infrastructure layer** -- making blockchains fast and cheap enough for mainstream use. Layer 2 solutions are the primary answer to this challenge.

## The Blockchain Trilemma

Coined by Vitalik Buterin, the **blockchain trilemma** states you can only optimize for two of three properties:

1. **Decentralization**: Many independent validators
2. **Security**: Resistant to attacks
3. **Scalability**: High transaction throughput

### The Trade-off
You can have any two, but not all three:
- Decentralization + Security = Low scalability (Ethereum L1)
- Security + Scalability = Centralization (traditional databases)
- Scalability + Decentralization = Weak security (some alt-chains)

Ethereum chose decentralization + security, sacrificing scalability.

## Layer 1 Limitations

The result of prioritizing decentralization and security:

- **~15 TPS** (transactions per second) compared to Visa's ~65,000 TPS
- During the 2021 NFT boom, simple transfers cost $50+ in gas fees
- Complex DeFi operations could cost $200+
- Network congestion made many use cases economically unviable

This wasn't a bug -- it was a deliberate design choice. But it meant a scalability solution was essential.

## How Layer 2 Works

The key insight is that **not every transaction needs full Layer 1 security**. Layer 2 solutions process thousands of transactions off-chain, then batch the results and post a single summary transaction to Layer 1.

### The Process
1. Thousands of transactions are processed on L2
2. Results are compressed into a **Rollup**
3. Only the compressed data is recorded on L1
4. L1 provides final verification -> L2 inherits L1's security

### The Result
- Fees dropped from $50 to ~$0.02
- Throughput increased to thousands of TPS
- L2 daily transaction volume now exceeds L1 by **10x or more**

**Analogy**: L1 is like the Supreme Court (slow, expensive, but ultimate authority). L2 is like local mediation (fast, cheap, handles most cases). Only disputes escalate to the Supreme Court.

## Optimistic Rollups vs ZK-Rollups

### Optimistic Rollups (Arbitrum, Base, Optimism)
"Innocent until proven guilty." Transactions are assumed valid. Anyone can submit a fraud proof within a ~7-day challenge window. If fraud is proven, the transaction is reverted.

### ZK-Rollups (zkSync, StarkNet)
"Prove your innocence upfront." Every batch includes a cryptographic validity proof (zero-knowledge proof). Math guarantees correctness with no challenge period needed -- instant finality.

| Aspect | Optimistic | ZK |
|--------|-----------|----|
| Withdrawal | ~7 days | Instant |
| Proof cost | Low | High |
| EVM compatibility | Easy | Harder |
| Maturity | More mature | Rapidly improving |

## Then & Now: Layer 2 Becomes Reality

### 2024 Landscape
- **Base** (Coinbase-backed) became the fastest-growing L2, capturing 55% of L2 transaction volume
- **Arbitrum** processed more transactions than Ethereum L1
- **Total L2 TVL** exceeded $40 billion

### The Dencun Upgrade (March 2024)
EIP-4844 introduced **blob transactions**, reducing L2 data costs by an additional 90%. This was the single biggest cost reduction in L2 history.

### Dixon's Prediction: Validated
Chris Dixon's 2018 prediction that "the next few years will focus on infrastructure layer construction" proved accurate. L2 infrastructure went from theoretical to handling the majority of Ethereum ecosystem transactions in just a few years.
key_ideas: Blockchain Trilemma: Decentralization, Security, Scalability — pick two
Layer 1 limitation: Ethereum ~15 TPS, Visa ~65,000 TPS
Layer 2 solution: Process transactions off-chain, post results to Layer 1
Optimistic Rollups vs ZK-Rollups: Different security assumptions
lessons.paper_ref: Chris Dixon, 'Why Decentralization Matters' (2018) / Ethereum L2 Docs
lessons.exercise:
What is the 'Blockchain Trilemma'?
1) The tradeoff between decentralization, security, and scalability
2) The conflict between on-chain governance, off-chain governance, and social consensus
3) The challenge of balancing transaction speed, gas costs, and smart contract complexity
4) The tension between privacy, transparency, and regulatory compliance on public chains
lessons.answer: The tradeoff between decentralization, security, and scalability
lessons.explanation: The Blockchain Trilemma (coined by Vitalik Buterin) states that a blockchain can only fully optimize two of three properties: decentralization, security, and scalability. Ethereum chose decentralization + security, resulting in only ~15 TPS. Layer 2 solutions break this trilemma by processing transactions off-chain while inheriting Layer 1's security, achieving thousands of TPS at a fraction of the cost.

lessons.title: DeFi and NFTs: The Blockchain Ecosystem
lessons.content:
## Blockchain Use Cases

With smart contracts (Ethereum) and scalability (Layer 2) in place, a rich ecosystem of applications has emerged. The major categories include:

- **Payments**: Cross-border transfers, micropayments
- **Store of Value**: Digital gold (Bitcoin)
- **DeFi**: Decentralized lending, trading, insurance
- **NFTs**: Digital ownership and creator monetization
- **DAOs**: Decentralized governance
- **CBDC**: Government-issued digital currencies

## DeFi (Decentralized Finance)

DeFi replaces traditional financial intermediaries (banks, brokers, insurers) with **smart contracts** that execute automatically.

### Key DeFi Protocols
- **MakerDAO** (rebranded to Sky in 2024): Collateralized lending -- deposit crypto, borrow stablecoins (DAI)
- **Uniswap**: Decentralized exchange (DEX) -- trade tokens without a centralized intermediary using Automated Market Makers
- **Aave**: Lending and borrowing with algorithmically adjusted interest rates

### DeFi Characteristics
- 24/7 operation (no bank hours)
- Permissionless access (no credit score required)
- Transparent (all transactions visible on-chain)
- Composable ("Money Legos" -- protocols stack together)

## Then & Now: DeFi's Rise and Fall and Recovery

### The Boom (2020-2021)
- DeFi TVL peaked at ~$180B in November 2021
- "DeFi Summer 2020" introduced yield farming and liquidity mining
- Protocols like Compound and Aave grew from millions to billions in TVL

### The Crash (2022)
- **LUNA/Terra collapse** (May 2022): Algorithmic stablecoin UST lost its peg, erasing $40B in value
- **Three Arrows Capital**: Major crypto hedge fund went bankrupt
- **FTX collapse** (November 2022): $8B in user funds vanished
- DeFi TVL crashed to ~$40B

### The Recovery (2023-2024)
- DeFi TVL recovering on L2 infrastructure
- Real-world assets (RWA) tokenization emerging as new growth area
- More sustainable yield models replacing speculative farming

## The FTX Lesson: "Not Your Keys, Not Your Coins"

FTX, the world's second-largest crypto exchange, collapsed in 2022 when it secretly lent user funds to its sister company Alameda Research, which lost the money.

### What Happened
- Sam Bankman-Fried used customer deposits as collateral for risky bets
- $8 billion in user funds vanished
- Users who trusted a **centralized custodian** lost everything

### The Irony
FTX collapsed because it was centralized -- the exact problem blockchain was designed to solve. In DeFi:
- Assets stay in YOUR wallet
- Smart contracts (not humans) execute trades
- Code is auditable and transparent
- No single person can misappropriate funds

**Analogy**: Keeping your crypto on an exchange is like storing gold in someone else's vault. If they secretly sell your gold, you have no recourse. Self-custody means keeping the gold in your own safe.

## NFT (Non-Fungible Token)

**Non-Fungible Tokens** prove unique digital ownership on blockchain. Unlike Bitcoin (fungible: 1 BTC = 1 BTC), each NFT is distinct.

### NFT History
- **CryptoKitties (2017)**: First mainstream NFT project, proved the concept
- **2021-2022 boom**: Beeple sold for $69M, BAYC became cultural phenomenon
- **Subsequent crash**: Speculative bubble burst, 90%+ of NFT collections lost value

### Beyond Speculation
NFTs as a technology remain viable for:
- **Creator monetization**: Artists receive royalties on secondary sales
- **Event tickets**: Verifiable, non-counterfeitable digital tickets
- **Digital credentials**: Diplomas, certifications, membership badges
- **Gaming assets**: True ownership of in-game items
- **Real-world asset tokenization**: Fractional ownership of real estate, art

## CBDC (Central Bank Digital Currency)

**Central Bank Digital Currencies** are government-issued digital money. They use similar technology but with opposite philosophy.

### Current State
- China's **e-CNY** is already in use by hundreds of millions
- The EU is developing the **digital euro**
- Over 130 countries are exploring CBDCs

### The Philosophical Tension
The same underlying technology (digital ledgers) can serve opposite purposes depending on who controls it:

| Aspect | Crypto | CBDC |
|--------|--------|------|
| Control | Decentralized | Government-controlled |
| Privacy | Pseudonymous | Fully traceable |
| Access | Permissionless | Permission required |
| Goal | Individual freedom | State monetary control |

Decentralized crypto prioritizes individual freedom; CBDCs prioritize state control. This tension will define the next era of digital finance.
key_ideas: DeFi (Decentralized Finance): Financial services without central institutions
NFT (Non-Fungible Token): Proof of digital ownership
CBDC (Central Bank Digital Currency): Government-issued digital money (centralized)
FTX collapse: Why decentralization matters — 'not your keys, not your coins'
lessons.paper_ref: Minhyun Kim, Common Computer — Blockchain Fundamentals Lecture Lesson 02
lessons.exercise:
What lesson does the FTX collapse teach about decentralization?
1) Centralized exchanges need stricter internal audits to prevent future collapses
2) DeFi protocols should implement circuit breakers to contain systemic risk from exchanges
3) 'Not your keys, not your coins' — centralized custody has counterparty risk
4) Regulators should mandate proof-of-reserves for all cryptocurrency platforms
lessons.answer: 'Not your keys, not your coins' — centralized custody has counterparty risk
lessons.explanation: FTX collapsed because it was a centralized custodian that secretly misappropriated $8 billion in user funds. Users who trusted FTX with their assets lost everything. In contrast, DeFi protocols keep assets in users' own wallets, with smart contracts (not humans) executing trades. The lesson is 'not your keys, not your coins' -- without private key control, a third party can lose or steal your assets. FTX's failure proved why the decentralization principles blockchain was built on actually matter.
